text: This Act shall be called the Pakistan Penal Code, and shall take effect throughout Pakistan.
Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Code and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within Pakistan.

Any persons liable, by any Pakistani Law, to be tried for an offence committed beyond Pakistan shall be dealt with according to the provisions of this Code as if such act had been committed within Pakistan.

The provisions of this Code apply also to any offence committed by any citizen of Pakistan or any person in the service of Pakistan in any place beyond Pakistan, and to offences committed on Pakistani ships or aircraft.

Nothing in this Act is intended to repeal, vary, suspend or affect any of the provisions of any Act for punishing mutiny and desertion of officers, soldiers, sailors or airmen in the service of the State or of any special or local law.
Throughout this Code every definition of an offence, every penal provision and every illustration shall be understood subject to the exceptions contained in the chapter entitled 'General Exceptions'.
Every expression which is explained in any part of this Code is used in every part of this Code in conformity with the explanation.
The pronoun 'he' and its derivatives are used of any person, whether male or female.
Unless the contrary appears from the context, words importing the singular number include the plural number, and words importing the plural number include the singular number.
The word 'man' denotes a male human being of any age; the word 'woman' denotes a female human being of any age.
This Act shall be called the Pakistan Penal Code, and shall take effect throughout Pakistan.
Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Code and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within Pakistan.

Any persons liable, by any Pakistani Law, to be tried for an offence committed beyond Pakistan shall be dealt with according to the provisions of this Code as if such act had been committed within Pakistan.

The provisions of this Code apply also to any offence committed by any citizen of Pakistan or any person in the service of Pakistan in any place beyond Pakistan, and to offences committed on Pakistani ships or aircraft.

Nothing in this Act is intended to repeal, vary, suspend or affect any of the provisions of any Act for punishing mutiny and desertion of officers, soldiers, sailors or airmen in the service of the State or of any special or local law.
Throughout this Code every definition of an offence, every penal provision and every illustration shall be understood subject to the exceptions contained in the chapter entitled 'General Exceptions'.
Every expression which is explained in any part of this Code is used in every part of this Code in conformity with the explanation.
The pronoun 'he' and its derivatives are used of any person, whether male or female.
Unless the contrary appears from the context, words importing the singular number include the plural number, and words importing the plural number include the singular number.
The word 'man' denotes a male human being of any age; the word 'woman' denotes a female human being of any age.
The word 'person' includes any Company or Association, or body of persons, whether incorporated or not.
The word 'public' includes any class of the public or any community.
The words 'servant of the State' denote all officers or servants employed in Pakistan by or under the authority of the Federal Government or any Provincial Government.
The word 'Government' denotes the persons authorized by law to administer executive Government in Pakistan or any part thereof.
The word 'Judge' denotes not only every person officially designated as a Judge, but also any person empowered by law to give a definitive judgment in any legal proceeding.
The words 'Court of Justice' denote a Judge or a body of Judges empowered by law to act judicially when acting judicially.
The words 'public servant' denote a person falling under specific categories such as military officers, judges, officers of court, and persons holding public office empowered by law.
'Moveable property' includes corporeal property of every description, except land and things attached to the earth.
'Wrongful gain' is gain by unlawful means of property to which the person gaining is not legally entitled, and 'Wrongful loss' is its inverse.
'Dishonestly' means doing anything with the intention of causing wrongful gain to one person or wrongful loss to another.
'Fraudulently' means doing a thing with intent to defraud but not otherwise.
'Reason to believe' implies sufficient cause to believe a thing, but not otherwise.
When property is in the possession of a wife, clerk or servant on account of another person, it is deemed to be in that person's possession.
'Counterfeit' means causing one thing to resemble another with intent to deceive or with knowledge that deception is likely.
'Document' denotes any matter expressed on any substance by means of letters, figures, or marks, intended to be used as evidence.
'Valuable security' is a document that creates, transfers, restricts, or extinguishes a legal right or acknowledges a legal liability.
'A will' denotes any testamentary document.
Words referring to acts include illegal omissions unless the context suggests otherwise.
'Act' includes a series of acts, and 'omission' includes a series of omissions.
When a criminal act is done by several persons in furtherance of the common intention, each is liable as if he alone committed it.
When a criminal act is done with criminal knowledge or intention by several persons, each is liable as if he acted alone with that knowledge or intention.
Causing an effect partly by act and partly by omission is the same offence as causing it wholly by act or wholly by omission.
Whoever intentionally co-operates in the commission of an offence by doing any one of several acts commits that offence.
Persons engaged in a criminal act may be guilty of different offences depending on their intention and knowledge.
'Voluntarily' means causing an effect by means intended or known to likely cause that effect.
'Offence' denotes a thing made punishable by this Code.
'Special law' means a law applicable to a particular subject.
'Local law' means a law applicable only to a particular part of Pakistan.
'Illegal' means anything contrary to law or forbidden by law. 'Legally bound to do' means a legal obligation to do something.
'Injury' means any harm illegally caused to body, mind, reputation, or property.
'Life' denotes the life of a human being, unless the contrary appears from the context.
'Death' denotes the death of a human being, unless the contrary appears from the context.
'Animal' denotes any living creature other than a human being.
'Vessel' denotes anything made for the conveyance by water of human beings or of property.
'Year' means a year reckoned according to the British calendar. 'Month' means a month reckoned according to the British calendar.
'Section' denotes one of those portions of a Chapter of this Code which are distinguished by prefixed numeral figures.
'Oath' includes a solemn affirmation substituted by law for an oath, and any declaration required or authorized by law to be made before a public servant.
'Good faith' means nothing is said to be done or believed in good faith which is done or believed without due care and attention.
'Harbour' means giving shelter or protection to a person with the knowledge that he has committed an offence or is wanted by law, unless legally bound to do so.
Punishments under this Code include: qisas, diyat, arsh, daman, ta’zir, death, imprisonment for life, imprisonment (rigorous or simple), forfeiture of property, and fine.
In all cases except where qisas has been awarded, the Federal Government may commute the sentence of death to any other punishment provided by this Code.
The Provincial Government may commute a sentence of imprisonment for life to imprisonment not exceeding 14 years.
Appropriate Government means the Federal Government for matters under its executive authority, and the Provincial Government for others.
The sentence of imprisonment shall not be commuted to a fine.
In calculating fractions of terms of punishment, imprisonment for life shall be reckoned as equivalent to imprisonment for 25 years.
Offenders sentenced to transportation before this Code's commencement shall be dealt with as if sentenced to rigorous imprisonment for the same term.
No sentence of imprisonment shall prescribe a particular form unless the law specifies rigorous or simple imprisonment.
If the offence is punishable with imprisonment but no type is specified, the Court may order either rigorous or simple imprisonment.
No person shall be sentenced to imprisonment for more than 14 years.
Where no sum is expressed to which a fine may extend, the amount is unlimited but not excessive.
If an offence is punishable with imprisonment and fine, the fine shall be in addition to imprisonment.
When an offender is sentenced to a fine only and fails to pay, he may be imprisoned for a term not exceeding the maximum provided for the offence.
If an offence is punishable with imprisonment and fine, and the offender is sentenced to pay a fine only but fails to pay, the imprisonment may extend up to one-fourth of the maximum imprisonment.
The imprisonment in default of fine shall terminate upon payment of the fine or when the full term has been served.

If the offence is punishable with fine only, the imprisonment in default shall not exceed two months if the fine does not exceed fifty rupees, or four months if it does not exceed one hundred rupees, or six months in any other case.
Imprisonment in default of fine is in addition to any other punishment imposed for the offence.
If any part of a fine is paid or recovered after imprisonment has begun, the term shall be reduced proportionately.
The fine may be levied at any time within six years from the sentence, or while the offender is still serving imprisonment in default.
If an act constitutes multiple offences, the offender may be punished for each, but not beyond the maximum punishment allowed for any one offence.
If an offence falls under two or more legal definitions, the offender may be punished under any applicable one.
Solitary confinement may be included in the sentence of rigorous imprisonment, but not exceeding three months in total and with breaks specified in the Code.
The term of solitary confinement shall not exceed 14 days at a time, with intervals of not less than 14 days between periods of such confinement.
Enhanced punishment may be awarded to offenders previously convicted of an offence punishable with three years or more of imprisonment, upon subsequent conviction of a similar offence.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is, or who by reason of a mistake of fact in good faith believes himself to be, bound by law to do it.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a Judge acting judicially in the exercise of his powers in good faith.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a person in pursuance of a judgment or order of a Court, even if the Court had no jurisdiction, provided the person believed it to have jurisdiction.
Nothing is an offence if done by a person who is justified or believes himself to be justified by law in doing it.
Nothing is an offence which is an accident or misfortune without criminal intention or knowledge, in the course of lawful conduct.
Nothing is an offence if it is done without criminal intent to avoid other harm to person or property, and the harm is justified in the context.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under seven years of age.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a child above seven and under twelve who has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge the nature and consequences of his conduct.
Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act.
Nothing is an offence done by a person involuntarily intoxicated, lacking capacity to understand the nature of the act.
In offences requiring intent or knowledge, voluntary intoxication is no excuse.
An act not intended to cause death or grievous hurt, done with consent of the person affected and not illegal, is not an offence.
An act not intended to cause death, done in good faith for a person's benefit with consent, is not an offence.
An act done in good faith for a child's benefit under 12 or a mentally ill person, by a guardian or with guardian's consent, is not an offence.
Consent is not valid if given under fear, misconception, or by a person incapable of understanding.
An act causing harm is not justified by consent if the harm is intended or known to cause death or grievous hurt.
An act done in good faith for the benefit of a person without consent due to inability to give it is not an offence, except causing death.
Communication made in good faith for a person’s benefit is not an offence, even if made without consent.
An act done under threat of instant death (except murder or offences against the State) is not an offence.
Acts causing slight harm are not offences.
Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.
Every person has the right of private defence of body and property.
Right of private defence applies even against persons of unsound mind, children, or intoxicated persons, if unlawful force is used.
No right of private defence against acts by public servants acting in good faith or under lawful authority.
The right of private defence of the body extends to causing death if there is danger of death, grievous hurt, rape, kidnapping, etc.
If the offence doesn't lead to death or grievous hurt, the right of private defence doesn't extend to causing death.
The right of private defence of body continues as long as danger exists.
The right of private defence of property extends to causing death in cases of robbery, house-breaking, mischief by fire, etc.
If the offence doesn't fall under section 103, the right of private defence of property doesn't extend to causing death.
The right of private defence of property continues until the threat ceases or possession is recovered.
If in exercising the right of private defence, harm is caused to an innocent person, it is not an offence if there is no safer alternative.
Abetment is the instigation, conspiracy, or intentional aiding of an offence.
A person may abet an offence even if incapable of committing the offence.
If no specific punishment is provided for abetment, the abettor shall receive the same punishment as for the offence abetted.
Abetment of an offence is punishable even if committed by a different class of person than the abettor.
An abettor is liable for the consequences of the act abetted, even if the final act is different from what was intended.
If the person abetted does a different act than abetted, the abettor is liable for both acts.
Abettor is liable when abetting an act known to be likely to cause a different offence.
When abettor is present at the time of the offence, he is treated as if he committed the offence himself.
Abetting an offence punishable with death or life imprisonment without it being committed is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Abetting an offence punishable with imprisonment without it being committed is punishable with one-fourth of the maximum term.
Abetting the commission of an offence by more than ten persons is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Concealing a design to commit an offence punishable with death or life imprisonment is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
A public servant concealing a design to commit an offence they are bound to prevent is punishable with up to 7 years or lesser punishment based on offence.
Anyone not being a public servant who conceals a design to commit an offence is punishable similarly under certain conditions.
'Criminal conspiracy' means an agreement between two or more persons to do an illegal act or a legal act by illegal means.
Punishment for criminal conspiracy depends on the nature of the act conspired: same as for the main offence or up to 6 months and fine otherwise.
Waging or attempting to wage war against Pakistan is punishable with death or life imprisonment and fine.
Conspiring to wage war against Pakistan or to overawe the Government by criminal force is punishable with life imprisonment or 10 years and fine.
Collecting arms with the intention of waging war against Pakistan is punishable with life imprisonment or 10 years and fine.
Concealing with intent to facilitate war against Pakistan is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Condemning creation of Pakistan or advocating abolishing its sovereignty is punishable with 10 years and fine.
Assaulting the President or Governor with intent to compel or restrain exercise of lawful power is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Sedition: Bringing hatred or contempt against the Government is punishable with life imprisonment or 3 years and fine.
Waging war against a foreign state at peace with Pakistan is punishable with life imprisonment or 7 years and fine.
Committing depredation on territories of a friendly state is punishable with up to 7 years and fine, and property may be forfeited.
Receiving property taken in depredation on territories of a friendly state is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Public servant voluntarily allowing prisoner of state or war to escape is punishable with life imprisonment or 10 years and fine.
Negligently allowing such prisoner to escape is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
Rescuing or aiding the escape of a state or war prisoner is punishable with life imprisonment or 10 years and fine.
Abetting mutiny in the armed forces is punishable with life imprisonment or 10 years and fine.
Abetment of mutiny if committed in consequence is punishable with death or life imprisonment and fine.
Abetment of assault by soldier on superior officer is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Abetment of assault by a soldier on a superior officer, when not committed, is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
Abetment of desertion of soldier, sailor or airman is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
Harbouring deserter is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
Deserter surrendering or apprehended is not punishable under section 136.
Abetment of insubordination is punishable with up to 6 months and fine.
Persons subject to military, naval or air force law are exempt from trial under this Code for same offence.
Wearing garb or carrying token used by soldier, sailor or airman is punishable with up to 3 months and fine.
An unlawful assembly is a group of five or more persons with a common objective to commit violence or resist law.
Whoever, being aware of facts, joins an unlawful assembly is considered a member.
Member of an unlawful assembly is punishable with up to 6 months and fine.
Joining an unlawful assembly armed with deadly weapon is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
Joining or continuing in unlawful assembly knowing it has been commanded to disperse is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
Use of force or violence by unlawful assembly or its members is called 'rioting'.
Punishment for rioting is up to 2 years and fine.
Rioting with deadly weapon is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
Every member of unlawful assembly is guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object.
Hiring or engaging persons to join unlawful assembly is punishable with the same punishment as for unlawful assembly.
Knowingly joining unlawful assembly after being hired is punishable with up to 6 months and fine.
Assaulting or obstructing public servant in suppression of riot is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
Wantonly giving provocation with intent to cause riot is punishable with up to 1 year and fine.
Promoting enmity between groups on grounds of religion, race, etc., is punishable with up to 5 years and fine.
Inducing students or others to boycott exams or public duties is punishable with up to 1 year and fine.
Every person aware of a design to commit a riot must inform the nearest police officer.
Owner of land where unlawful assembly or riot is committed is bound to give information to police.
Negligent owner or occupier allowing assembly or riot on premises is punishable with fine.
Harbouring persons hired for unlawful assembly is punishable with up to 6 months and fine.
Being hired to take part in unlawful assembly is punishable with up to 6 months and fine.
'Affray' is when two or more persons fight in a public place disturbing public peace.
Punishment for affray is up to 1 month and fine.
Public servant taking gratification other than legal remuneration in respect of official act is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
Taking gratification to influence public servant is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
Taking gratification to screen offender is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
Punishment for abetment by public servant is equal to offence abetted.
Public servant obtaining valuable thing without consideration is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
Punishment for offering gratification to a public servant is the same as for taking it.
Public servant disobeying law intentionally to cause injury is punishable with up to 1 year and fine.
Public servant framing incorrect documents to cause injury is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
Public servant engaging in trade is punishable with fine only.
Public servant unlawfully buying or bidding for property is punishable with fine.
Personating a public servant is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
Wearing garb used by a public servant with fraudulent intent is punishable with up to 3 months and fine.
Definitions relating to elections — candidate, electoral right, etc.
Bribery at elections is punishable with up to 1 year and fine.
Undue influence at elections is punishable with up to 1 year and fine.
Personation at elections is punishable with up to 1 year and fine.
Punishment for bribery and undue influence is up to 1 year and fine.
Punishment for personation at elections is up to 1 year and fine.
False statements in election context are punishable with fine.
Illegal payments in election context are punishable with fine.
Failure to keep election account is punishable with fine.
Absconding to avoid service of summons or other proceeding is punishable with up to 1 month and fine.
Preventing service of summons or preventing publication is punishable with up to 1 month and fine.
Non-attendance in obedience to an order from a public servant is punishable with up to 1 month and fine.
Omission to produce document or electronic record is punishable with up to 1 month and fine.
Omission to give notice or information to a public servant is punishable with up to 1 month and fine.
Furnishing false information is punishable with up to 6 months and fine.
Refusing oath when duly required is punishable with up to 6 months and fine.
Refusing to answer a public servant is punishable with up to 6 months and fine.
Refusing to sign a statement is punishable with up to 3 months and fine.
False statement on oath is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
False information to cause a public servant to use lawful power to the injury of another is punishable with up to 6 months and fine.
Resistance to lawful apprehension or escape is punishable with up to 6 months and fine.
Obstructing sale of property offered for sale by authority of public servant is punishable with imprisonment up to one month, or fine up to five hundred rupees, or both.
Illegal purchase or bid for property offered for sale by authority of public servant is punishable with imprisonment up to one month, or fine up to five hundred rupees, or both.
Obstructing public servant in discharge of public functions is punishable with imprisonment up to three months, or fine up to five hundred rupees, or both.
Omission to assist public servant when bound by law to give assistance is punishable with imprisonment up to one month, or fine up to two hundred rupees, or both.

Disobedience to order duly promulgated by public servant is punishable with simple imprisonment up to one month and/or fine up to two hundred rupees; if such disobedience causes danger to human life, health or safety, the punishment may extend to six months.
Threat of injury to public servant is punishable with imprisonment up to two years, or fine, or both.
Threat of injury to induce a person to refrain from applying for protection to a public servant is punishable with imprisonment up to one year, or fine, or both.
Giving false evidence is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years, and fine.
Fabricating false evidence is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years, and fine.
Punishment for false evidence in judicial proceeding is up to seven years; in other cases, up to three years and fine.
Giving or fabricating false evidence with intent to procure conviction of capital offence is punishable with imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to ten years and fine.
Giving or fabricating false evidence to procure conviction of an offence punishable with life imprisonment or not less than seven years is punishable with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.
Using evidence known to be false is punishable as if the person gave false evidence.
Issuing or signing false certificate is punishable with imprisonment up to three years and fine.
False statement in declaration which is by law receivable as evidence is punishable with imprisonment up to three years and fine.
Using as true a certificate or declaration known to be false is punishable as if the person gave false evidence.
Using as true a declaration known to be false in a judicial proceeding is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and fine.
Causing disappearance of evidence of offence or giving false information to screen offender is punishable depending on the severity of the main offence.
Intentionally omitting to give information of offence by person legally bound to inform is punishable with imprisonment up to six months or fine or both.
Giving false information respecting an offence is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine or both.
Destruction of document to prevent its production as evidence is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine or both.
False personation to cause a summons or proceeding is punishable with imprisonment up to three years and fine.
Fraudulent removal or concealment of property to prevent its seizure is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine or both.
Fraudulent claim to property to prevent seizure is punishable with imprisonment up to two years and fine.
Fraudulently suffering decree for sum not due is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine or both.
False claim in Court is punishable with imprisonment up to two years and fine.
Fraudulently obtaining decree for sum not due is punishable with imprisonment up to two years and fine.
False charge of offence made with intent to injure is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and fine.
Harbouring offender whose punishment is death is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and fine; lesser punishment for lesser offences.
Taking gift, etc., to screen offender from punishment is punishable with up to seven years or fine depending on the offence.
Offering gift or gratification to screen offender is punishable similar to section 213.
Taking gift to help recover stolen property without informing police is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine or both.
Harbouring offender is punishable with varying terms depending on the underlying offence.
Harbouring robbers or dacoits is punishable with rigorous imprisonment up to seven years and fine.
Public servant disobeying law to save person from punishment is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine or both.
Public servant framing incorrect record to save person from punishment is punishable with imprisonment up to three years or fine or both.
Public servant making report contrary to law to save person from punishment is punishable with imprisonment up to three years and fine.
Commitment for trial or confinement by public servant contrary to law is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and fine.
Intentional omission to apprehend person by public servant is punishable with imprisonment depending on the offence of the person.
Intentional omission to apprehend person under sentence or lawfully committed is punishable with varying terms based on severity.
Escape from confinement negligently suffered by public servant is punishable with simple imprisonment up to two years or fine or both.
Resistance or obstruction to lawful apprehension is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine or both.
Resistance or obstruction to lawful apprehension or rescue in case of capital offence is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and fine.
Resistance or obstruction to lawful apprehension of another person is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine or both.
Omitted.
Violation of condition of remission of punishment is punishable with the original punishment for which remission was granted.
Intentional insult or interruption to public servant during judicial proceeding is punishable with simple imprisonment up to six months or fine or both.
Personation of assessor is punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine or both.
Definition of 'coin' includes stamped metal used as money.
Counterfeiting coin is punishable with imprisonment for life or up to ten years and fine.
Counterfeiting Pakistan coin is punishable with imprisonment for life or up to ten years and fine.
Making or selling instruments for counterfeiting coin is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years and fine.
Making or selling instrument for counterfeiting Pakistan coin is punishable with imprisonment up to life or 10 years and fine.
Possession of instrument or material for counterfeiting coin is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Abetting in Pakistan the counterfeiting out of Pakistan of coin is punishable as if the act were committed in Pakistan.
Import or export of counterfeit coin is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Import or export of counterfeiting instruments is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Delivery of counterfeit coin as genuine, which was known to be counterfeit, is punishable with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
Delivery of counterfeit Pakistan coin as genuine, which was known to be counterfeit, is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Delivery of counterfeit coin as genuine, which was not known to be counterfeit when received, is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Possession of counterfeit coin by person who knew it to be counterfeit when he became possessed thereof is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Possession of counterfeit Pakistan coin known to be counterfeit is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Person employed in mint causing coin to be of different weight or composition is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Unlawfully taking coining instrument from mint is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Fraudulently diminishing weight or altering composition of coin is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Fraudulently diminishing weight or altering composition of Pakistan coin is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Altering appearance of coin with intent that it shall pass as a different coin is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Altering appearance of Pakistan coin is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Delivery of coin possessed with knowledge that it is altered is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Delivery of altered Pakistan coin with knowledge is punishable with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
Possession of coin altered with knowledge is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Possession of altered Pakistan coin with knowledge is punishable with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
Delivery of coin as genuine which, when received, was not known to be altered is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Counterfeiting Government stamp is punishable with imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine.
Having possession of instrument for counterfeiting Government stamp is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Making or selling instruments for counterfeiting Government stamp is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Sale of counterfeit Government stamp is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Having counterfeit Government stamp is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Using as genuine a known counterfeit Government stamp is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Effacing writing from substance bearing Government stamp with intent to cause loss is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Using Government stamp known to have been before used is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Erasure of mark denoting that stamp has been used is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Prohibition of fictitious stamps; making, using, or possessing is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years and fine.
Fraudulent use of false instrument for weighing is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Fraudulent use of false weight or measure is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Being in possession of false weight or measure is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Making or selling false weight or measure is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Public nuisance defined: any act causing common injury, danger or annoyance to the public.
Negligent act likely to spread infection of disease dangerous to life is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Malignant act likely to spread infection of disease dangerous to life is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Knowingly disobeying quarantine rule is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Adulteration of food or drink intended for sale is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Sale of noxious food or drink is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Adulteration of drugs is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Sale of adulterated drugs is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Sale of drug as a different drug is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Fouling water of public spring or reservoir is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 months or fine or both.
Making atmosphere noxious to health is punishable with fine up to five hundred rupees.
Rash driving or riding on a public way is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Rash navigation of vessel is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Exhibition of false light, mark, or buoy is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Conveying person by water for hire in unsafe or overloaded vessel is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Exhibition of dangerous object in public place is punishable with fine up to two hundred rupees.
Negligent conduct with respect to poisonous substance is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Negligent conduct with respect to fire or combustible matter is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Negligent conduct with respect to explosive substance is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Negligent conduct with respect to machinery is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Negligent conduct with respect to pulling down or repairing buildings is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Negligent conduct with respect to animal is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Public nuisance not otherwise punishable is punishable with fine up to six hundred rupees.
Continuance of nuisance after injunction to discontinue is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Sale, etc., of obscene books, etc., is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 months or fine or both.
Sale, etc., of obscene objects to young person under age 20 is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Obscene acts and songs in public are punishable with imprisonment up to 3 months or fine or both.
Keeping lottery office or promoting lottery is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Injuring or defiling place of worship with intent to insult religion is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Deliberate and malicious acts intended to outrage religious feelings are punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years or fine or both.
Disturbing religious assembly is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Trespassing on burial places with intent to wound religious feelings is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Uttering words with deliberate intent to wound religious feelings is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Use of derogatory remarks against holy personages is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Misuse of epithets, descriptions and titles, etc., reserved for holy personages or places is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Person of Ahmadi group calling himself a Muslim or preaching or propagating his faith is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Definition of 'Qatl' (homicide), 'Diyat', 'Qisas', etc., for offences affecting the human body.
Qatl-i-Amd (intentional murder) defined as causing death with intention or knowledge.
Qatl-bis-sabab (causing death without intention but through unlawful act) is defined.
Punishment for intentional murder includes Qisas, Diyat, imprisonment for life or death penalty depending on the case.
Qatl liable to Qisas shall not be liable when offender is a minor, insane or commits the offence in good faith mistake.
Punishment for qatl not liable to Qisas includes imprisonment up to 25 years.
Definition of Wali (legal heir) who is entitled to claim Qisas.
Qatl liable to Qisas is excluded in cases of minor, insane, and lineal ascendants.
Attempt to commit qatl-i-amd (intentional murder) is punishable with same punishments as for the offence.
Qatl committed under ikrah (compulsion) is punishable with imprisonment up to 25 years.
Waiver (Afw) of Qisas by Wali is permitted.
Compounding of Qisas (Sulh) allowed through agreement and payment of Diyat.
Court may still punish in public interest even after waiver or compounding.
Causing miscarriage is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Causing miscarriage without woman's consent is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Death caused by act done with intent to cause miscarriage is punishable with Qisas or Diyat.
Act done with intent to prevent child being born alive is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years.
Qatl-i-amd of unborn child defined and punishable.
Exposure and abandonment of child under 12 years is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years.
Qatl-i-khata (manslaughter) defined as unintentional causing of death.
Punishment for Qatl-i-khata is Diyat and imprisonment up to 5 years if committed by rash or negligent act.
Shajjah (injury to head or face) is defined and classified.
Punishment of Shajjah is Qisas or Daman depending on severity.
Itlaf-i-salahiyyat-i-udw (destroying function of organ) defined and punishable.
Punishment for Itlaf is Qisas or Daman.
Attempt to commit murder by causing hurt is punishable with up to 10 years or more depending on the case.
Voluntarily causing grievous hurt is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Voluntarily causing hurt by dangerous weapons is punishable with up to 14 years and fine.
Voluntarily causing hurt to extort confession or property is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Causing hurt by means of poison or any substance is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Causing hurt by means of fire or heated substance is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Definition of Diyat and rules for its payment in cases of hurt or homicide.
Daman means compensation determined by court for causing hurt without causing death.
Hurt is defined as bodily pain, disease or infirmity.
Causing grievous hurt voluntarily is punishable with Qisas, Diyat or Daman.
Punishment for Itlaf-i-udw (destroying or impairing any limb or organ of a person) is Qisas, and where not applicable, Diyat or imprisonment up to 10 years.
Punishment for Itlaf-i-salahiyyat-i-udw (impairing function of organ) is Qisas, and where Qisas is not applicable, Diyat or imprisonment up to 10 years.
Punishment for causing hurt by means of corrosive substance is imprisonment up to 14 years and fine not less than one million rupees.
Punishment for causing hurt by rash or negligent act is imprisonment up to 5 years and Daman.
Definitions of different kinds of hurt like Shajjah and their punishments based on severity (e.g., Shajjah-i-khafifah to Shajjah-i-amah).
Punishment for Itlaf-i-salahiyyat-i-udw without Qisas is Daman and imprisonment up to 10 years.
Punishment for causing Itlaf of hearing or sight or smell, taste or voice is Diyat.
Punishment for causing Itlaf of reproductive organs is Diyat.
Punishment for fracture or dislocation of bone is Daman and imprisonment.
Punishment for various types of hurt not liable to Qisas includes imprisonment and Daman.
Punishment for hurt by rash or negligent act is imprisonment up to 5 years and Daman.
Punishment for hurt not falling under above definitions is imprisonment and Daman.
Definition of Diyat and its amount is fixed in accordance with Islamic injunctions and current market value.
Causing hurt by administering substance with intent to harm is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and Daman.
Causing miscarriage without woman’s consent is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and Diyat.
Causing death of unborn child is punishable with Diyat.
Isqat-i-Haml (abortion) without reasonable cause is punishable with up to 3 years and Diyat.
Abortion is allowed only under threat to life of mother; otherwise, it is an offence.
Wrongful restraint is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 month or fine up to 1,500 rupees or both.
Wrongful confinement is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine up to 3,000 rupees or both.
Punishment for wrongful restraint is imprisonment up to 1 month or fine or both.
Punishment for wrongful confinement is imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Wrongful confinement for more than 24 hours is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years and fine.
Wrongful confinement for 3 or more days is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Wrongful confinement for extortion of property is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Wrongful confinement in secret is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years in addition to other punishments.
Wrongful confinement to extort confession is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Wrongful confinement by public servant to extort confession is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Force is defined as intentionally causing motion or change in motion of another person.
Criminal force is defined as intentionally using force without consent in order to cause fear, injury or annoyance.
Assault is defined as threat or attempt to apply criminal force without consent.
Punishment for assault or criminal force otherwise than on grave provocation is imprisonment up to 3 months or fine or both.
Assault or criminal force to deter public servant from duty is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Assault or criminal force to woman with intent to outrage modesty is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Assault or stripping of woman in public is punishable with death or life imprisonment and fine.
Assault with intent to dishonor person otherwise than on grave provocation is punishable with up to 2 years or fine or both.
Assault in attempt to commit theft of property carried by person is punishable with up to 2 years or fine or both.
Assault or criminal force in attempt to wrongfully confine a person is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Assault or criminal force on grave provocation is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 month or fine or both.
Kidnapping is defined as conveying a person beyond the limits of Pakistan without consent, or unlawful taking of minor or insane person.
Kidnapping from Pakistan is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Kidnapping from lawful guardianship is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Abduction is defined as compelling a person by force or deceit to go from any place.
Punishment for kidnapping is imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Kidnapping or abducting with intent to murder is punishable with death or life imprisonment and fine.
Kidnapping for ransom is punishable with death or life imprisonment and fine.
Kidnapping or abducting with intent to secretly and wrongfully confine is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Kidnapping or abducting for extortion is punishable with death or life imprisonment and fine.
Kidnapping, abducting or inducing woman to compel marriage is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Procuration of minor girl is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Importation of girl below 21 years of age is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Kidnapping or abducting in order to subject person to grievous hurt or slavery is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Wrongfully concealing or keeping in confinement a kidnapped or abducted person is punishable with same punishment as the original offence.
Kidnapping or abducting child under 10 years with intent to steal from its person is punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years and fine.
Buying or disposing of any person as a slave is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Habitual dealing in slaves is punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years and fine.
Selling minor for purposes of prostitution is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Buying minor for prostitution is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Unlawful compulsory labor is punishable with imprisonment up to 5 years or fine or both.
Definition of rape and circumstances under which it is committed.
Punishment for rape is death or imprisonment not less than 10 years and fine.
Unnatural offences are punishable with imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine.
Theft is defined as taking property out of possession without consent.
Punishment for theft is imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Theft in dwelling house, vessel or building is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Theft by clerk or servant is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Theft after preparation for causing death or hurt is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Extortion is defined as intentionally putting someone in fear to deliver property or valuable security.
Punishment for extortion is imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Putting person in fear of injury in order to commit extortion is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Extortion by putting a person in fear of death or grievous hurt is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Putting person in fear of death or grievous hurt to commit extortion is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Extortion by threat of accusation of offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Putting person in fear of accusation to commit extortion is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Robbery is defined as theft or extortion with violence or threat of immediate violence.
Dacoity is robbery committed by five or more persons conjointly.
Punishment for robbery is imprisonment up to 10 years and fine; if robbery is committed on highway, punishment may extend to 14 years.
Attempt to commit robbery is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Voluntarily causing hurt in committing robbery is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Punishment for dacoity is imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine.
If any one of five or more persons commits murder during dacoity, all are punishable with death, life imprisonment, or up to 10 years and fine.
Robbery or dacoity with attempt to cause death or grievous hurt is punishable with minimum 7 years.
Preparation to commit dacoity is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Making preparation to commit dacoity is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Belonging to a gang of persons associated for dacoity is punishable with imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine.
Belonging to a gang associated for habitual theft is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Assembling for purpose of committing dacoity is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Dishonest misappropriation of movable property is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Dishonest misappropriation of property of a deceased person is punishable with up to 3 years or 7 years depending on the offender's role.
Criminal breach of trust is defined as dishonest misappropriation by a person entrusted with property.
Punishment for criminal breach of trust is imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Criminal breach of trust by carrier, warehouse-keeper or wharfinger is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Criminal breach of trust by clerk or servant is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Criminal breach of trust by public servant or banker is punishable with imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine.
Stolen property includes property obtained by theft, extortion or robbery.
Dishonestly receiving stolen property is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Receiving stolen property knowing it was taken during dacoity is punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years and fine.
Habitually dealing in stolen property is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Assisting in concealment or disposal of stolen property is punishable with up to 3 years or fine or both.
Cheating is defined as deceiving someone to deliver property or do something they wouldn't otherwise do.
Cheating by personation is punishable under same rules as cheating.
Punishment for cheating is imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Cheating with knowledge that wrongful loss may ensue to person whose interest the offender is bound to protect is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
Punishment for cheating by personation is imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Dishonest or fraudulent removal or concealment of property to prevent distribution among creditors is punishable with up to 2 years or fine or both.
Dishonest or fraudulent prevention of debt or demand due to be available for creditors is punishable with up to 2 years or fine or both.
Dishonest or fraudulent execution of deed of transfer containing false statement is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
Dishonest or fraudulent removal or concealment of property is punishable with up to 2 years or fine or both.
Mischief is defined as causing destruction or change to property with intent to cause loss or damage.
Punishment for mischief is imprisonment up to 3 months or fine or both.
Mischief causing loss or damage of 50 rupees or more is punishable with up to 2 years or fine or both.
Mischief by killing or maiming animal valued at 10 rupees or more is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Mischief by killing or maiming cattle or animals of value over 50 rupees is punishable with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
Mischief by injury to public road, bridge, river or channel is punishable with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
Mischief by injury to telegraph or railway line or work is punishable with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
Mischief by causing inundation or obstruction to public drainage is punishable with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
Mischief by destroying, moving or rendering less useful a lighthouse or seamark is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Mischief by destroying or moving land-mark fixed by public authority is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Mischief by fire or explosive substance with intent to cause damage over 100 rupees is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Mischief by fire or explosive substance with intent to destroy house, etc. is punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years and fine.
Mischief with intent to destroy vessel is punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years and fine.
Attempt to commit mischief by fire or explosive substance is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Punishment for mischief described in Sections 437 and 438 if committed by fire or explosive substance may extend to life imprisonment.
Mischief committed after preparation for causing death, hurt, or wrongful restraint is punishable with up to 5 years and fine.
Criminal trespass is defined as unlawful entry onto property with intent to commit an offence, or to intimidate or annoy the occupier.
House-trespass is defined as criminal trespass into any building used for dwelling or custody of property.
Lurking house-trespass is defined as house-trespass secretly or by hiding presence.
Lurking house-trespass by night is defined as lurking house-trespass committed after sunset and before sunrise.
House-breaking is defined as house-trespass with specific means of entry or exit like scaling wall, breaking locks, etc.
House-breaking by night is defined as house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise.
Punishment for criminal trespass is imprisonment up to 3 months or fine up to 1,500 rupees or both.
Punishment for house-trespass is imprisonment up to 1 year or fine up to 2,000 rupees or both.
House-trespass to commit offence punishable with death is punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years and fine.
House-trespass to commit offence punishable with imprisonment for life is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
House-trespass to commit offence punishable with imprisonment is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
House-trespass after preparation for causing hurt or assault is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Punishment for lurking house-trespass or house-breaking is imprisonment up to 2 years and fine.
Lurking house-trespass or house-breaking to commit offence is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
Lurking house-trespass or house-breaking after preparation to cause hurt or fear is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Punishment for lurking house-trespass by night or house-breaking by night is imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Lurking house-trespass or house-breaking by night to commit offence is punishable with up to 5 years and fine.
Lurking house-trespass or house-breaking by night after preparation to cause hurt or fear is punishable with up to 14 years and fine.
Hurt caused during lurking house-trespass or house-breaking is punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years and fine.
Death or grievous hurt caused during lurking house-trespass or house-breaking is punishable with death, life imprisonment or up to 10 years and fine.
Dishonestly breaking open receptacle containing property is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
Same offence committed after preparation to cause hurt or fear is punishable with up to 5 years and fine.
Forgery is defined as making false documents with intent to cause damage or support a claim or cause injury.
Definition of making a false document including signing, altering or concealing documents dishonestly or fraudulently.
Punishment for forgery is imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Forgery of court record or public register is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Forgery of valuable security, will or authority to adopt son is punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years and fine.
Forgery for purpose of cheating is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Forgery for purpose of harming reputation is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
A forged document is called a false document.
Using a forged document as genuine is punishable with same punishment as for forgery.
Making counterfeit seals for forging court records is punishable with life imprisonment or up to 7 years and fine.
Making counterfeit seals for general forgery is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Possessing forged documents with intent to use as genuine is punishable with life imprisonment or up to 7 years and fine.
Counterfeiting seals or documents for purpose of forgery is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Counterfeiting seals used by courts or public offices is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Forgery of account books or valuable security is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Falsification of accounts by clerk or servant is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years or fine or both.
Cancelled.
Cancelled.
Using a false trademark is punishable with fine up to 2,000 rupees.
Using false property mark is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Punishment for using a false property mark is imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Counterfeiting a property mark used by another is punishable with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Counterfeiting a property mark used by a public servant is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
Making or possessing instruments for counterfeiting property mark is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Selling goods marked with counterfeit property mark is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Making a false mark upon any receptacle containing goods to mislead is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Punishment for using false mark upon receptacle is imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.
Cancelled.
Counterfeiting currency notes or bank notes is punishable with imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine.
Using or delivering counterfeit currency notes is punishable with imprisonment for life or up to 10 years and fine.
Possessing counterfeit currency notes is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Making or possessing instruments or materials for forging currency notes is punishable with up to 20 years and fine.
Making or using documents resembling currency notes is punishable with fine up to 1,000 rupees.
Cancelled.
Breach of contract to attend on and supply necessaries to helpless person is punishable with imprisonment up to 3 months or fine or both.
Breach of contract to marry is punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.
Man deceitfully inducing a woman to believe herself lawfully married is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
Cohabitation caused by a man deceitfully inducing a belief of lawful marriage is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Marrying again during lifetime of husband or wife is punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
Same offence with concealment of former marriage is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Marriage ceremony fraudulently gone through without lawful marriage is punishable with up to 7 years and fine.
Enticing or taking away or detaining with criminal intent a married woman is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Adultery has been repealed and is no longer an offence.
Enticing or taking away or detaining with criminal intent a married woman is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
Prohibition of forced marriage is punishable with imprisonment up to 10 years or fine or both.
Prohibition of depriving woman from inheriting property is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Prohibition of using deceitful means to deprive woman from inheritance is punishable with up to 10 years and fine.
Defamation is defined as making or publishing an imputation intended to harm a person's reputation.
Punishment for defamation is imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.
Printing or engraving matter known to be defamatory is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
Sale of printed or engraved substance containing defamatory matter is punishable with up to 2 years and fine.
Criminal intimidation is defined as threatening a person to cause alarm or compel to do or omit an act.
Intentional insult with intent to provoke breach of peace is punishable with up to 2 years or fine or both.
Statements conducing to public mischief are punishable with up to 7 years or fine or both.
Punishment for criminal intimidation is imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both; with death threat up to 7 years.
Criminal intimidation by anonymous communication is punishable with up to 2 years in addition to other punishments.
Act caused by inducing a person to believe they will be rendered an object of divine displeasure is punishable with up to 1 year or fine or both.
Word, gesture or act intended to insult the modesty of a woman is punishable with up to 3 years and fine.
Misconduct in public by a drunken person is punishable with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.
Attempt to commit offences punishable with life imprisonment or other imprisonment is punishable with same as if offence had been committed.
